title: Non-uniqueness of smooth solutions to the Navier-Stokes equations on torus T^2

thm:1.1.summary: MAIN RESULT: The 2D Navier-Stokes system on $T^2$ admits two distinct global smooth solutions u, ũ $\in C^\infty_{t,x}(R^+ \times T^2)$ with the same initial data in $BMO^{-1}(T^2)$. Settles the 2D non-uniqueness problem left open by Coiculescu-Palasek (who solved the 3D case).

rem:1.2.summary: Notes that the method extends to $R^2$ by combining with the iterative scheme from [MNY-ar1], [MNY-ar2].

prop:2.1.summary: INDUCTIVE STEP: Given m-1 smooth NS solutions satisfying uniform $BMO^{-1}$ bounds, constructs $w_m = w^(p)_m + w^(s)_m + w^(ns)_m$ such that $u_m$ :$= u_{m-2} + w_m$ also solves NS with ‖$u_m$‖$_{L^\infty_t BMO^{-1}} \le C_0$. The decomposition ensures even/odd subsequences share initial data but diverge for t > 0.

prop:3.1.summary: Basic $C^N$ bounds on the amplitude functions $a_{(k,2q)}$ and building blocks $\varphi_{k,2q}$. Amplitudes satisfy ‖$a_{(k,2q)}$‖$_{L^\infty_t C^N}$ ≲ $C_0^{1/2}$ ℓ_{2q-1}^{-N}, building blocks ‖$\varphi_{k,2q}$‖$_{C^N}$ ≲ $\lambda_{2q}^{N/4}$.

prop:3.2.summary: Key estimates for the primary flow w^(p)_{2q} and inverse-cascade flow w^(s)_{2q} in $L^\infty_t C^N$ and $L^1_t C^N$ norms. Controls the Reynolds stress R w^(p)_{2q} and provides the inductive bounds needed for the iteration.

prop:3.3.summary: $L^2_t L^r \cap L^1_t W^{1,r}$ estimates for w^(p)_{2q} and w^(s)_{2q}. Crucially exploits support shrinkage: $|\Omega$̃$_{2q-1}| \le 2^{-(2q-1)}|T^2|$, yielding the geometric decay ‖w^(p)_{2q}‖ $\le C_0^{3/4} 2^{-2q/r}$ needed for Cauchy convergence.

prop:3.4.summary: $BMO^{-1}$ accumulation bound: ‖$\Sigma_{l=1}^q (w^(p)_{2l} + w^(s)_{2l})$‖$_{L^\inftyBMO^{-1}} \le (1/16)C_0 \Sigma_{m=-1}^{2q} 2^{-m}$. Ensures the running sum of oscillatory and cascade flows stays within the $BMO^{-1}$ budget.

prop:3.5.summary: Self-interaction error estimate: decomposes div(w^(p)_{2q} ⊗ $w^(p)_{2q}) + \partial_t w^(s)_{2q} = F^(1)_{2q} + \nablaP^(1)_{2q}$. The heat-dominated Fourier mode design absorbs the main oscillatory quadratic term into pressure, leaving ‖F^(1)_{2q}‖$_{L^1_t B^{-1/2}_{\infty,1}}$ ≲ $C_0 \lambda_{2q-1}^{-15}$.

prop:3.6.summary: Cross-interaction and dissipation error: bounds F^(2)_{2q} (containing $(\partial_t - \Delta)w^(p)_{2q}, \Deltaw^(s)_{2q}$, and all bilinear cross-terms) in $L^1_t B^{-1/2}_{\infty,1} \le C_0^2 \lambda_{2q}^{-1/4}$ and $L^1_t B^0_{\infty,1} \le C_0^2 \lambda_{2q}^{1/4}$.

prop:3.7.summary: Perturbation flow construction: obtains global-in-time $w^(ns)_{2q} \in C^\infty$ solving forced NS with forcing $-F_{2q}$ and zero data. Works in subcritical Besov space $B^{-1/2}_{\infty,1} (notBMO^{-1}$ as in CP), achieving ‖w^(ns)_{2q}‖ $\le \lambda_{2q-1}^{-10}$ via Gronwall + transport-diffusion theory.

def:A.1.summary: Standard mollifying kernels $\varphi(t) \in C^\infty_c(-1,0)$ and $\psi(x) \in C^\infty_c(B_1(0))$ and their $\varepsilon-scaled$ versions $\varphi_\varepsilon, \psi_\varepsilon$.

lem:A.2.summary: Stationary 2D Euler flows from [CDS]: for $\Lambda \subseteq S^1 \cap Q^2$ with $-\Lambda = \Lambda$, the vector field $W(\xi) = \Sigma b_k (ibar{k}/|k|) e^{ik\cdot\xi}$ is real-valued, divergence-free, and satisfies div(W⊗$W) = (1/2)\nabla(|W|^2 - |...|^2)$. This is the geometric core enabling the heat-dominated flow construction.

lem:A.3.summary: Geometric lemma from [CDS]: there exist $\Lambda \subseteq S^1 \cap Q^2$ and smooth functions $a_k$: $B_{10^{-3}}(Id) \to$ R such that R $= \Sigma a_k^2(R)$ bar{k}⊗bar{k} for each symmetric matrix R near Id. Enables the amplitude decomposition in the convex integration scheme.

def:A.4.summary: Nonhomogeneous Besov space $B^s_{p,q}(T^2)$ via Littlewood-Paley decomposition: ‖u‖$_{B^s_{p,q}} =$ ‖(2^{js}‖$\Delta_j$ u‖$_{L^p})_j$‖_{ℓ^q}.

def:A.5.summary: Mixed time-spatial Besov spaces L̃$^r_T B^s_{p,q}$: ‖u‖ = ‖(2^{js}‖$\Delta_j$ u‖$_{L^r_T L^p})_j$‖_{ℓ^q}. The Chemin-Lerner refinement used for the perturbation flow estimates.

lem:A.6.summary: Transport-diffusion estimate from [BCD11]: for divergence-free v, solutions of $\partial_t$ u $- \Deltau + v\cdot\nablau =$ g satisfy ‖u‖_{L̃$^\rho_T B^{s+2/\rho}_{p,1}} \le$ C exp(C $V_p(T))($‖$u_0$‖$_{B^s_{p,1}} +$ ‖g‖_{L̃$^{\rho_1}_T B^{s-2+2/\rho_1}_{p,1}})$. Central tool for the perturbation flow w^(ns)_{2q}.

prop:A.7.summary: Oscillatory $BMO^{-1}$ estimate: ‖f $e^{i\lambdak\cdotx}$‖$_{L^\infty_t B^{-1}_{\infty,\infty}}$ ≲ $\lambda^{-1}$‖f‖$_{L^\infty_t L^\infty} + \lambda^{-2}$‖f‖$_{L^\infty_t C^2}$. Converts high-frequency oscillation into $B^{-1}_{\infty,\infty}$ smallness; used throughout the error estimates.

edges.note: Proof of Thm 1.1 (Section 4) directly invokes Prop 2.1 to construct two sequences ${u_{2q}}$ and ${u_{2q+1}}$, then uses the estimates (2.13) for Cauchy convergence in $L^2_t L^r \cap L^1_t W^{1,r}$.
Section 4 uses the support-shrinkage argument from Prop 3.3 to show $w^(p)_{2q+1} \to 0$ in $W^{-1,p}(T^2)$, proving the two limits share initial data.
Inductive step requires the $C^N$ estimates for w^(p)_m and w^(s)_m from Prop 3.2 to verify bounds (2.5)-(2.10).
Inductive step requires $L^2_t L^r \cap L^1_t W^{1,r}$ decay from Prop 3.3 to establish (2.13).
$BMO^{-1}$ accumulation bound from Prop 3.4 ensures ‖$u_m$‖$_{L^\infty_t BMO^{-1}} \le C_0$ at each step.
Prop 3.5 controls the self-interaction error F^(1)_{2q}, which feeds into the total forcing $F_{2q}$ absorbed by w^(ns)_{2q}.
Prop 3.6 controls the cross-interaction error F^(2)_{2q}, the other component of the total forcing.
Prop 3.7 constructs w^(ns)_{2q} absorbing all error terms, completing the inductive step.
Proof of Prop 3.2 uses amplitude and building block bounds from Prop 3.1 to estimate ‖Rw^(p)_{2q}‖$_{L^\infty_t C^N}$ and ‖w^(p)_{2q}‖$_{L^1_t C^N}$.
Support properties of $\varphi_{k,2q}$ from Prop 3.1 give $|\Omega_j| \le 4^{-j}|T^2|$, driving the geometric $L^r$ decay.
Uses the Reynolds stress bound ‖Rw^(p)_{2q}‖$_{L^\infty_t C^N}$ from Prop 3.2 and the inductive $C^N$ bounds.
Converts oscillatory products into $BMO^{-1}$ smallness via the oscillatory estimate in Prop A.7.
Stationary Euler flow identity from Lemma A.2 decomposes div(W⊗W) into pressure gradient plus controlled remainder — the key cancellation enabling the heat-dominated construction.
Geometric Lemma A.3 provides the amplitude decomposition R $= \Sigma a_k^2$ bar{k}⊗bar{k} used to construct the Reynolds stress cancellation.
Amplitude bounds from Prop 3.1 used to estimate F^(1,1)_{2q} and F^(1,2)_{2q} in Besov norms.
Oscillatory $BMO^{-1}$ estimate converts high-frequency quadratic terms to $B^{-1}_{\infty,\infty}$ bounds, e.g. ‖F^(1,1)_{2q}‖$_{L^1_t B^{-1}_{\infty,\infty}}$ ≲ $C_0 \lambda_{2q}^{-3/4}$.
Uses $C^N$ bounds for w^(p)_{2q} and w^(s)_{2q} from Prop 3.2 to bound bilinear cross-terms in F^(2)_{2q}.
Oscillatory estimate used to bound $(\partial_t - \Delta)w^(p,main)_{2q}$ in $B^{-1}_{\infty,\infty}$, yielding (3.19).
Transport-diffusion estimate from Lem A.6 applied to the forced NS system for w^(ns)_{2q}, giving the $B^{-1/2}_{\infty,1}$ bound (3.26).
The forcing $F_{2q} = F^(1)_{2q} + F^(2)_{2q}$; the F^(1)_{2q} bound from Prop 3.5 feeds into the right-hand side of (3.27).
The F^(2)_{2q} bound from Prop 3.6 feeds into the right-hand side of (3.27).
Uses $L^1_t C^1$ bounds for $w^(p)_{2q} + w^(s)_{2q} + u_{2q-2}$ from Prop 3.2 and inductive hypothesis in the Gronwall argument (3.30).

layer_1_statement_and_context.description: Main theorem statement and context for the 2D $BMO^{-1}$ non-uniqueness problem

layer_2_iterative_framework.description: Inductive scheme constructing sequences $u_{2q}, u_{2q+1}$ via three-component decomposition $w_m = w^(p)_m + w^(s)_m + w^(ns)_m$

layer_3_building_blocks.description: Amplitude functions, building blocks, and their $C^N$ and $L^r$ estimates

layer_4_error_analysis.description: Quadratic self-interaction error F^(1) (heat-dominated cancellation) and cross-interaction error F^(2) in Besov spaces

layer_5_perturbation_closure.description: Global perturbation flow w^(ns)_{2q} absorbing all errors via forced Navier-Stokes in subcritical Besov space

layer_6_analytic_toolbox.description: Foundational tools: stationary 2D Euler flows, geometric decomposition, Besov spaces, transport-diffusion, oscillatory estimates
